data.title: Fastly: The Quant Score Says Buy, The Fundamentals Say Hold
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Fastly, Inc. (FSLY) is a San Francisco-based edge cloud platform. The stock did very well in FY2025, surging to highs of ~$35 for a roughly 600% move from its 52-week low. Last year was the company's first non-GAAP profitable fiscal year, with revenue of $624 million (up 15% YoY) and guidance for FY2026 of $700-$720 million in revenue and $50-$60 million in non-GAAP operating income. Net revenue retention climbed to 110%. RPO grew 55% YoY to $353.8 million. The cybersecurity business grew 21%. After years of negative cash flow and investor worries about customer concentration, Fastly finally seems to have turned the corner.

But there is something that gives us pause: the current valuation. At about 8.3x trailing P/S, Fastly trades at a meaningful premium to Akamai (AKAM) (~4x) while growing three times faster, but at a steep discount to Cloudflare (NET) (~27x forward P/S), which grows twice as fast. Fastly's value likely falls between the two comps. The market is betting on the Cloudflare-type arc. We are not convinced. AI Accelerator and semantic caching must prove they can move the revenue needle. Until they do, we see the risk-reward as balanced. We rate Fastly a HOLD.

What Fastly Does

Fastly operates a global edge cloud platform. It runs a distributed network of servers at the perimeter of the internet, pushing content delivery, security enforcement, and increasing computation as close to end users as possible. If a user in Tokyo loads a web page, Fastly's edge node in Tokyo serves it, not a data center in Virginia.

Network services represented $477.8 million of FY2025 revenue, or about 78% of the total. Security products, including web application firewall, DDoS protection, and bot management, contributed $125.1 million (20%). A smaller "other" category covers "emerging compute" and AI products and added $21.1 million, up 64% YoY. That last bucket is small but worth watching.

Fastly started as a content delivery network (CDN) company built for developers. That focus has shifted. Now the company leads with Compute@Edge, a serverless platform based on WebAssembly (Wasm) that lets customers run software at the network edge with decreased latency and increased efficiency. The 2026 growth strategy leans heavily on AI Accelerator, a semantic caching layer meant to cut the cost of running large language model inference at the network edge. Finding new customers, or at least converting existing customers at scale, will drive the investment case over the next 12-18 months.

As of Q4 2025, no single customer exceeds 10% of revenue. Total customer count stood at 3,092, with 628 enterprise customers spending an average of roughly $900,000 annually. That is up from 596 enterprise customers a year earlier.

Financials

FY2025 was a great year for Fastly.

Revenue hit $624 million, up 15% from $543.7 million in FY2024. The final months saw continued momentum; Q4 revenue grew 23% YoY, reaching $172.6 million, the company's fastest quarterly growth rate in years.

Margins improved as well; GAAP gross margin hit 61.4% in Q4, showing an increase in higher-margin security and compute offerings.

Fastly reported $19.7 million in non-GAAP net income for FY2025, its first full year of positive non-GAAP earnings. The prior year showed a $12.1 million non-GAAP loss. FY2026 guidance of $50-$60 million in non-GAAP operating income shows continued margin expansion is expected.

Despite improvement, Fastly still reported a GAAP net loss of $121.7 million for FY2025. Stock-based compensation of $117.3 million constitutes the majority of the deviation between GAAP and non-GAAP results. Until GAAP profitability is achieved, the company cannot technically be considered profitable.

Operating cash flow reached $94.4 million, reversing years of negative or sparse cash flow. After $28.7 million in capex, free cash flow was about $65.7 million. R&D spend was $162.7 million, or ~26% of revenue.

The company ended FY2025 with $180.6 million in cash and equivalents and $181.2 million in short-term investments. Total debt stood at $430.2 million, of which $323.3 million is long-term. That puts net debt at $68.4 million and the Debt/Equity ratio at 0.46.

Valuation

This is where our concern lies. Shares currently trade around $34, giving the company a market cap of roughly $5.2 billion. The most recent surge was driven by the appointment of marketing executive Joan Jenkins as CMO. The 52-week low was $4.87, so shares have appreciated over 600% and now sit at the 52-week high of $34.82. SA's aggregate Wall St. analyst score shows a 3.50/5 Buy rating, SA analysts rate it 3.83/5 Buy, and the SA Quant rating is Strong Buy at 4.99/5. That discrepancy between quant momentum and fundamentals tells us that technical traders love this stock, but the analysts covering it have not revised their models to account for the recent rally.

At current levels, the valuations are stretched. Trailing P/S is ~8.3x. EV/Sales (TTM) is ~8.4x, and forward EV/Sales is ~7.3x based on consensus FY2026 revenue of $712 million. Investors are paying for accelerated growth and an AI edge vertical that has not been proven.

Peer Comparison

Company Mkt Cap EV FY25 Rev Rev Growth Gross Margin EV/Rev (TTM) Fwd P/S
Akamai (AKAM) $17.4B $21.1B $4.21B 5% ~60% ~5x ~4x
Cloudflare (NET) $74.5B $73.9B $2.17B 30% 75% ~34x ~27x
Fastly (FSLY) $5.2B $5.2B $624M 15% 57% 8.3x 7.1x

Fastly sits in the uncomfortable middle ground between two very different peers, each representing a compelling story for investors.

Cloudflare is the growth story, with $2.17 billion in FY2025 revenue, 30% growth, 75% gross margins, and a market cap 15x the size of Fastly. The valuation gap is wide (~27x forward P/S versus Fastly's ~7x), but Cloudflare's scale, growth rate, and customer base justify the premium. Cloudflare is already building the type of platform that Fastly aspires to become.

Akamai is the value story, with $4.21 billion in revenue, strong profitability, and a forward P/E of ~17x. Akamai trades at roughly 4x forward P/S. The market sees Akamai as a mature, commoditizing business. Fastly's 7x P/S multiple implies investors see it as closer to Cloudflare than Akamai, but 15% revenue growth does not quite justify that view.

Market consensus prices Fastly as a high-growth edge-AI software play, not a commodity CDN vendor. At its current growth rate, that remains aspirational.

The Bull Case

Network-edge AI is what currently drives the stock price. Fastly's AI Accelerator, slated for wider rollout this fiscal year, uses semantic caching to cut the cost of running LLM inference at the edge. Enterprises could choose edge-based inference for savings in cost or latency over centralized inference. Fastly has a head start with its global PoP footprint and Wasm-based compute. The issue is that nobody has proven the market exists at scale yet.

The more concrete growth lever is security. The segment grew 21% in FY2025 and now makes up 20% of revenue. AI Bot Management, launched in 2025, targets a real and growing problem for enterprises dealing with AI scrapers. This product is scalable, has real growth potential, and should help expand margins.

Net revenue retention at 110% in Q4 2025, up from 106% in Q3, shows that existing customers are spending more. RPO growth of 55% YoY to $353.8 million shows strong forward visibility. Enterprise customer count grew 5% to 628. Revenue from the company’s ten largest customers increased 28%, a positive signal for customer satisfaction.

Forrester named Fastly a Leader in edge development platforms in Q1 2026, which should help with enterprise sales cycles. The company's Compute@Edge platform, built on Wasm, offers near-zero cold starts that AWS Lambda@Edge cannot match. If Wasm adoption accelerates in the developer community, Fastly's early bet on the technology could pay off.

Risks

The stock trades at ~$34 at the 52-week high of $34.82, and analysts do not think there is much upside left. SA's quant rating aggregator gives the Valuation factor a D grade, even as it rates the overall stock a Strong Buy on momentum and growth. That tension between technicals and fundamentals is the single largest risk in our view.

Non-GAAP profitability is a big accomplishment for Fastly, but the GAAP accounting reflects a net loss of $121.7 million for FY2025. For investors who care about GAAP earnings (they should), Fastly is still a GAAP net-loss operation, with a $5 billion market cap. A company trading at 8x sales needs to be speeding up, not slowing down. One guidance miss in 2026 would compress the multiple fast.

Cloudflare's revenue is 3.5x Fastly's and growing at 30% versus Fastly's 15%. Both companies compete for enterprise edge and security budgets. Cloudflare's larger scale and higher margins give it more room to invest in R&D, undercut competitors, and bundle services. In an environment where Fastly and Cloudflare are competing for the same business, Cloudflare currently has the advantage. Fastly's main advantage is developer experience and Wasm performance, but that advantage will likely narrow over time.

AWS CloudFront, Azure CDN, and Google Cloud CDN are sufficient for most customers and often come included with or heavily discounted as part of larger cloud infrastructure contracts. Fastly's niche is a differentiator, but many enterprises that are committed to a hyperscaler have little incentive to add a standalone CDN vendor.

AI Accelerator and semantic caching sound great in theory, but the question we struggle with is, will enterprises actually route LLM inference through Fastly when they could run it centrally through OpenAI, Anthropic, or their hyperscaler of choice? If the AI product line does not deliver, Fastly remains a 15% YoY growth CDN and security operation trading at nearly 8x sales.

Conclusion

Fastly's management has done a great job turning the business around. Operating cash flow hit $94.4 million, gross margins are expanding, and the business is nearing GAAP profitability. Diversification of their client base and revenue mix is a welcome development, and the security vertical is growing.

Our hesitation comes down to fundamentals. The 600%+ rally from the 52-week low has left the stock trading in risky territory. At $34, the market cap is well ahead of the underlying business. A company growing revenue at ~15% and generating $19.7 million in non-GAAP profit does not typically command a $5 billion market cap unless the market sees an acceleration that the Street has not yet modeled. Also worth noting, the FY2026 growth strategy leans heavily on AI Accelerator. While the product has long-term potential, it is still unproven and lacks a clear, scalable market in the near term.

At these prices, we cannot justify the underlying valuation. For now, we have to wait and see how things shake out from the sidelines. If Q2 and Q3 show AI Accelerator generating revenue, and the growth rate pushes above 20%, current multiples become more tenable.

At Avalon Capital Research, we rate Fastly, Inc. a HOLD.

Investment Approach

  • Fidelity® Limited Term Municipal Income Fund (FSTFX) is a diversified national municipal bond strategy investing in general obligation and revenue- or tax-backed municipal securities across the short-intermediate part of the yield curve.
  • Our investment approach focuses on fundamental credit analysis, yield-curve positioning and an analysis of the structural characteristics of each security.
  • The fund's interest rate sensitivity is targeted closely to that of its benchmark to prevent interest rate speculation from overwhelming research-based strategies that we deem to have a higher likelihood of long-term success.
  • We emphasize a total-return approach that seeks to generate a high level of tax-exempt income, consistent with the preservation of capital.

Municipal Market Review

Tax-exempt municipal bonds capped off 2025 with a resilient fourth quarter, sustaining the momentum they gained in the prior three months as interest rates declined, tax-backed credit fundamentals remained solid, and demand ticked higher. The Bloomberg Municipal Bond Index gained 1.56% (including interest payments and price changes) for the quarter, helping munis offset a volatile first half of 2025 and lifting their full-year advance to 4.25%.

Munis notched most of their Q4 gain in October (+1.24%). That month, the U.S. Federal Reserve reduced its benchmark policy interest rate by a quarter percentage point (25 basis points), marking the central bank's second such rate cut in as many months, aimed at supporting a softening job market.

Improving technical factors were an additional tailwind. On the supply side, the pace of new bond issuance decelerated. Meanwhile, demand for munis strengthened, particularly among investors looking to lock in yields ahead of further anticipated interest-rate cuts. Moreover, passage of the One Big Beautiful Bill Act in July preserved the tax-exempt status of municipal bonds, removing a headwind that had created uncertainty in the muni market during the first half of 2025.

Munis then largely held steady in November (+0.23%) and December (+0.09%), supported by another quarter-point Federal Reserve rate reduction in December. Although new-issue volume remained elevated and contributed to a record year for issuance, it slowed toward year-end amid the typical seasonal lull in new deals. Supply was largely absorbed without market disruption amid firming investor demand, as healthy yields continued to attract buyers' attention.

Credit fundamentals for most tax-backed munis remained solid throughout the three months, with default rates very low and issuers' balance sheets generally healthy.

For the full quarter, long-duration municipals led the market, with 10-, 15-, 20- and 22-plus-year bonds posting the top gains (+1.74%, +2.84%, +2.20% and 1.63%, respectively), while one-, three-, five- and seven-year munis advanced 0.56%, 0.46%, 0.50% and 0.83%, respectively.

From a sector perspective, revenue bonds (+1.57%) performed roughly in line with general obligation bonds (+1.60%). Among GOs, local-government debt (+1.71%) modestly outpaced state-backed bonds (+1.44%). In the revenue category, there was little differentiation between the performance of various segments or ratings. Housing (+1.77%) and hospital bonds (+1.75%) fared best, while most other sectors followed closely behind – except for industrial development bonds, which returned only 0.65%.

By credit-quality rating, BBB-rated bonds gained 1.77%, slightly bettering returns in the 1.50% range notched by munis with higher ratings (AAA, AA, A).

Outlook and Positioning

As of December 31, yields on municipal bonds are still relatively high historically, offering tax-equivalent yields that compare favorably with U.S. Treasuries and corporate bonds, particularly for investors in high tax brackets.

As for market technicals, we believe new-issue supply may continue to remain high as issuers increase their borrowing due to higher costs and uncertainty around future federal funding. Against that backdrop, we believe investor demand will need to remain strong to absorb this higher level of expected issuance.

It is important for investors to remember that Fidelity's municipal bond portfolios are constructed with a careful and intentional emphasis on issue selection, especially with consideration to the liquidity of the security and the financial resiliency of its issuer. In the current environment, we continue to evaluate each fund holding and are monitoring those that may be more financially challenged than others.

Going forward, and as always, we will seek to identify opportunities to purchase municipal securities that offer both attractive valuations and a solid risk/reward profile. At quarter end, we continue to invest for strong risk-adjusted returns, while remaining cautious with yield-curve positioning, given the likelihood of ongoing interest-rate volatility.

We remain committed to the approach of building individual exposures in the portfolio that reflect risks with which we are comfortable, at entry prices we believe offer strong relative value. ■

CHARACTERISTICS

Duration is a measure of a security's price sensitivity to changes in interest rates. Duration differs from maturity in that it considers a security's interest payments in addition to the amount of time until the security reaches maturity, and also takes into account certain maturity shortening features (e.g., demand features, interest rate resets, and call options) when applicable. Securities with longer durations generally tend to be more sensitive to interest rate changes than securities with shorter durations. A fund with a longer average duration generally can be expected to be more sensitive to interest rate changes than a fund with a shorter average duration.

30-day SEC Yield is a standard yield calculation developed by the Securities and Exchange Commission for bond funds. The yield is calculated by dividing the net investment income per share earned during the 30-day period by the maximum offering price per share on the last day of the period. The yield figure reflects the dividends and interest earned during the 30-day period, after the deduction of the fund's expenses. It is sometimes referred to as "SEC 30-Day Yield" or "standardized yield".

30-Day SEC Restated Yield is the fund's 30-day yield without applicable waivers or reimbursements, stated as of month-end.

Medicare Contribution tax is a tax on non-municipal investment income that applies to individuals with incomes over $200,000 (or $250,000, filing jointly). For state-specific funds, TEY is based not only on the highest federal tax rate (40.8%) but also the highest state tax rate. For state-specific funds, TEYs assume investors are state residents and would not be able to take an itemized deduction on their federal returns for state taxes on investment income. For NY funds, TEYs do not reflect the NY state tax rate that applies to income in excess of $5 million. For MD funds, TEYs reflect the highest city/county tax rates in MD and treat them the same as state taxes. Consult a tax professional for further detail.

determined by taking the total assets of a fund, subtracting the total liabilities, and dividing by the total number of shares outstanding.

data.title: Rigel Pharmaceuticals Was Easy To Dismiss - Now It's Hard To Ignore (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Overview

My past hesitation in Rigel Pharmaceuticals (RIGL) was twofold: financial and operational.

Financially, Rigel was a microcap stock with a dwindling cash reserve. Operationally, Tavalisse seemed no more than a niche product. And although Rezlidhia had just launched, it didn’t appear to have a place in AML.

However, I did acknowledge some signs a turnaround. Losses were narrowing and partnerships with Eli Lilly (LLY) de-risked pipeline aspirations.

I ended with a cautious “Hold” rating.

RIGL has since gone up 140%.

I take another look below.

Recent Developments

After acquiring the US rights from Gavreto from Blueprint Medicines in early 2024, Rigel now makes money from three products. Gavreto, FDA-approved for both RET+ metastatic NSCLC and RET+ advanced thyroid cancer, generated $42.1M in FY25. Meanwhile, Rigel’s legacy products, Tavalisse and Rezlidhia, brought in $158.8M and $31M, respectively.

Although revenues were up 64% YoY in FY25, profitability did not suffer. Rigel's gross profit margin (82.02%) and EBITDA margin (43.46%) outperform its peers in the Health Care sector. And, in the trailing twelve months, Rigel generated $75.7M in cash from operations.

This is likely attributed to the "tuck-in" nature of Gavreto. Rigel's existing salesforce already had plenty of experience with hematologists/oncologists. So it ended up fitting right in without much added cost.

Moving on to pipeline developments, R289 is currently in a Phase 1b study for lower-risk MDS. Rigel presented encouraging data at ASH last year. 33% (6/18) of transfusion-dependent patients achieved transfusion independence, or TI, on R289.

Ocadusertib (Eli Lilly), a RIPK1 inhibitor, recently completed enrollment for the Phase 2 study in rheumatoid arthritis. Eli Lilly did exit the CNS arm of its Rigel collaboration last year.

Gavreto

Gavreto has become a “preferred” first-line option in the NCCN Guidelines for RET fusion-positive metastatic NSCLC.

Eli Lilly's Retevmo, Gavreto's primary competitor, maintains slightly higher efficacy numbers in untreated patients (cross-trial limitations aside). But Gavreto, with a 38.8 month median DOR, appears to have an edge in pre-treated patients.

RET fusion-positive only accounts for ~1-2% of total NSCLC cases, but it’s safe to assume that Gavreto’s peak share in this niche market could be much larger than Blueprint expected when they out-licensed it.

My discounted cash flow model (Gavreto in NSCLC) assumes 2031 Peak, 2036 LOE, 2,050 Target Pop., 30% Peak Share, 0.5% annual Pop. Growth, 80% Ownership Split, $180k Net Price, 10% COGS, 25% Steady-State OpEx, and a Competitor Impact of 10% beginning in 2028 (potential impact from next-gen RET inhibitors).

I have Gavreto generating a little over $80M in peak annual revenue. It is worth roughly $245M in Net Present Value.

Rezlidhia & Tavalisse

Tavalisse’s demand has exploded in ITP, although policy tailwinds (i.e., the elimination of the Medicare Part D coverage gap) in FY25, expected to subside in FY26, played some part.

Tavalisse is typically viewed as one of four options in second-line treatment for ITP. It is particularly popular among patients who want to avoid immunosuppression (i.e., rituximab) or surgery (i.e., splenectomy), which is more people than you'd think.

Recent real-world data highlighted Tavalisse’s effectiveness in elderly and comorbid patients who cannot tolerate the thrombotic risks associated with TPO-RAs (the third “other” second-line treatment option).

Tavalisse has carved a niche in ITP and figures to remain a steady contributor.

Rezlidhia is dominated by Tibsovo in IDH1 AML. Rigel is hoping to expand Rezlidhia into glioma (Phase 2) and front-line combos.

Pipeline

R289’s 33% TI rate may not sound like much, but it’s pretty impressive when you consider the population (salvage).

Dose-expansion data, expected later this year, hopes to, at the least, maintain this 30%+ TI rate.

R289 is the first IRAK1/4 inhibitor to show meaningful activity in MDS. Because it targets a unique pathway (the innate immune system), it may be a good candidate for combination therapy in the future, which would push it into earlier lines.

R289 comes with two major competitive advantages over competitors like SC Reblozyl and IV Rytelo:

  1. It is an oral pill.
  2. It has shown a manageable safety profile (Grade 1/2 GI effects).

When I model R289, its risk-adjusted NPV falls near 0. So, I suspect that further de-risking is needed (i.e., advance to Phase 2/3 trial; combination studies) for the stock to really begin to feel its impact.

FY26 Guidance

Rigel ended FY25 with a healthy $155M in cash and investments.

Rigel guides for between $275M and $290M in FY26 revenue. The FY26 guidance requires nuance, though.

After accounting for the $40M Rigel booked last year in non-cash revenue from the collaboration adjustment, management’s midpoint still calls for 12% organic revenue growth.

Because R289 is still years away from a launch, especially as management is carefully enrolling patients (slow and steady), it’s apparent that Rigel’s business could use a boost.

During the call, management expressed a desire for a new late-stage asset by 2028. So I think they’re feeling some pressure to sign a deal this year to ensure they have a new launch in the 2027/2028 window, lest revenue growth stall.

Rigel also signaled (“we’re not looking at expanding the sales organization at this point in time,” “very targeted efforts”) that they are more interested in squeezing every penny from a “Gavreto dollar” than they are expanding its reach.

Conclusion

Overall, I like what I am seeing from Rigel. I think they have some flexibility with regard to chasing growth and/or improving margins.

Gavreto has really paid off. And it increases confidence in management’s ability to strike gold again. Meanwhile, R289 and the Eli Lilly partnership keep things interesting “under the hood.” And now that Rigel is cash generative with a few established products, its stock does not feel as risky as it did before.

So I’m comfortable with upgrading my rating on RIGL to a speculative “Buy.”

Risks to investment include Tavalisse overdependence, competition, clinical trial failures, pricing pressures, regulatory headwinds, and management missteps.

data.title: Exail Technologies: The Growth Story For This Defense Tech Winner Is Far From Over
data.description:
Exail Technologies (EXALF) has gained 16.4% since my last report, outperforming the S&P 500’s 5.3% decline. In my view, the performance fits Exail’s combination of innovative edge in fiber optics, marine robotics, and autonomous submarines with a strong positioning to benefit from increasing demand from Europe as part of the push from Europe for increased security and defense autonomy. Europe will likely remain dependent on U.S. defense solutions for years to come, but with friction between Europe and its longtime ally reaching new highs, European defense security companies are increasingly better positioned to capitalize on increases in defense budgets. In this report, I briefly discuss Exail’s activities, the FY25 results, and the outlook for 2026 and update my price target in support of my buy rating.

Exail is a European Leader in Fiber Optics and Autonomous Systems

Exail was formed in 2022 when ECA Group and iXblue merged, combining autonomous and marine robotics and navigation and photonics solutions in a single company. I consider it a merger of two complementary technology portfolios rather than a merger of two companies.

The company offers surface drones as well as underwater drones, inertial navigation, sonars, acoustic positioning equipment, and quantum instruments. That means that Exail does not only have system and subsystem expertise but also parts expertise. Especially for intelligence, surveillance, and reconnaissance solutions, I do believe the company’s autonomous solutions are positioned extremely well. We also note that its fiber optic solutions and navigation products can be applied outside of the sea and underwater domains with potential applications in the space and land domains. So, there is a major expansion opportunity for Exail Technologies.

The risks for Exail include the fact that it always needs to invest to maintain a competitive edge and it has to scale up production and avoid targeting growth that would stress the fragile aerospace and defense supply chains too much. The company is also dependent on defense budget cycles and faces competitive pressures.

An Impressive Year for Exail

In 2025, Exail’s revenues grew 28% to €479 million, with 21% growth in advanced technology sales and 29% growth in navigation and maritime robotics sales. EBITDA grew 40% to €103 million, showing an EBITDA margin improvement from 20% to 22%, while operating income increased 75% to €32 million.

What we see is that the company is seeing strong EBITDA margins, and I believe that this is only the start of the EBITDA margin expansion path, as higher volumes supported by robust demand provide strong and increasing operating leverage.

The company had an order intake of €844 million during the year, indicating a book-to-bill ratio of 1.8x, while the backlog reached €1.1 billion or 2.3x years of sales. That is not a backlog coverage that allows for an acceleration in annual sales growth, but it should at the very least be extremely supportive of a constant growth rate of around 20%, which is substantial. One thing that we should keep in mind is that around half of the order intake came from the Middle East in 2025 for the Mine Countermeasure drone. There are two ways to look at this. One way is that with the Middle East being at war right now, demand may further increase. The other way to look at it is that GDP growth substantially diminished purchasing power.

Exail Targets Further EBITDA Margin Improvement

Exail does not quantify its targets for 2026, but it does expect double-digit growth in sales for 2026, while EBITDA is expected to grow faster than revenues, with the objective to reach 25% in EBITDA margins when large new programs start ramping up. What we will keep in mind is what program ramp-ups do with the margins. The nature of many engineering projects and ramp-ups is that in the initial phase, the margins are dilutive to the composite margins of the company. That also means that Exail has to nail the execution.

Exail Technologies Looks Very Attractive

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

Note: Price targets are based on Exail’s listing at the Euronext Paris. Price targets are converted from EUR to USD.

Exail is trading substantially ahead of its median EV/EBITDA. However, we see that the stock is trading nearly perfectly in line with the peer group and in line with the earnings performance of 2025. If we pull in 2026 earnings, we see 31% upside with a $192 price target and even 81% upside when we factor in earnings for 2027. So, I remain bullish on the stock. We note that EBITDA was 6.7% better than expected in 2025, while for 2026 and 2027 the estimates have increased by 2.1% and 3.8% for a total lift to the estimates of 3%. Free cash flow was substantially better than anticipated, with a $75 million free cash flow, whereas $36.5 million was expected. This is likely driven by cash collection on order placement and milestone achievements on contracts. For 2026, the free cash flow estimate has improved 6.6% but was revised down by 1.8% for 2027.

Through 2028, strong growth is expected with 21.2% sales growth, nearly 30% EBITDA growth, and 15% growth in free cash flow as the company’s capital expenditures are also expected to rise and operating cash flow during the year was probably extraordinarily strong prior to the company ramping up its business.

For Exail, the expectation is that margins will increase this year, remain flat in 2027, and then improve substantially by 2028. Free cash flow is expected to be down this year as capital expenditures increase, but as the business ramps up, the free cash flow should curl up again with significant free cash flow growth in 2028. Capital intensity against revenues is expected to increase this year and then moderate in the years after, as CapEx against operating cash flow will also moderate. The company increased its debt last year and earlier this year, but as its free cash flow generation starts to accelerate, we should see the company entering a net cash position, which is a very good position to be in.

Conclusion: Exail Looks Impressive

Exail Technologies has an extremely promising concept, as it puts its innovative strength as the through value builder. It is a defense tech company that translates its know-how into a competitive edge in a market with growing demand and creates value for shareholders in the process rather than focusing on shareholder value and operating the business with shareholder value generation as a cornerstone. I believe when engineering and expertise are used as the cornerstone of a company, the returns for shareholders can be outsized compared to taking the approach in the other direction. What also helps Exail is that its operating leverage should start to substantially improve in the years ahead while its margins already look impressive.

data.title: Undercovered Dozen: CVR Energy, Greystone Housing, Conagra Brands, And More
data.description:
The Undercovered Dozen is a weekly Seeking Alpha editor-curated series highlighting 12 articles on lesser-covered stocks from the previous seven days. We hope this provides ideas and inspires discussion among the community.

Today, we're looking at articles published between March 27 and April 2.

Take a look at what these less-covered stocks might hold for you. And please join the conversation below to share what you think: Are any of these worth following up on? And are there other undercovered ideas that you like?

CVR Energy Poised To Soar

Publius | Strong Buy

CVR Energy (CVI) is a small oil refiner with refineries in Kansas and Oklahoma, and it also owns significant pipeline and logistics systems as well as a 37% interest in a nitrogen fertilizer business (publicly traded CVR Partners - ticker UAN). Over the past few years, I believe CVI has made numerous poor decisions, resulting in less than optimal performance. But a combination of new management, changes that have already been implemented, government action, and the recent bottoming of the refining cycle is about to lead to spectacular performance for their business. To top that off, the wars in Ukraine/Russia and Iran/Middle East should supercharge their earnings for at least the rest of this year.

Read more here.

Market's Impatience Is Growing My Income: Greystone Housing

Rida Morwa | Buy

Greystone Housing Impact Investors LP (GHI), yielding 11.1%, is a unique partnership that invests in multi-family housing using two distinct strategies.

At Q3 2025 earnings, management telegraphed a dividend cut. We observed:

"Given the commentary, the last seems unlikely. We think something around $0.25 is probably favored, but we also wouldn't blame management if they just decide to bring the distribution back to 2020 levels. It might be better to be conservative and increase the distribution with each sale, than to risk sitting here a year from now with no properties sold and having to cut again."

Here we sit about 5 months later, and none of the JV (Joint Venture) properties have been sold, and apparently, the outlook is that none are going to be sold soon. GHI has decided to cut its distribution even more aggressively to $0.14/unit.

Arguably, it would have been better for optics if they had just slashed it enough last year, instead of choosing a level that required cash from their JV strategy. No doubt the market will be on edge for a while until GHI proves that the bottom is in. Ripping the band-aid off quickly probably would have been better than the path of multiple cuts.

There is no question that in terms of communication, management dropped the ball. But we aren't interested in great communication, we are interested in making money.

Read more here.

Conagra Brands: A+ Valuation, 9% Dividend, Lowest Price Since 2009 = Strong Buy

Paul Franke | Strong Buy

My regular readers know I have been quite optimistic about undervalued food stocks since last summer. Most have continued to slide lower in price. The biggest problem for many has been stagnating sales in the U.S. economy tied to rising GLP-1 weight loss drug popularity (and investor fears of lower food consumption growth overall in the years ahead). Packaged and processed foods are being purchased at lower growth rates, while fresh fruits and vegetables have actually witnessed better trends.

Another problem for the sector has been a jump in short selling across the board. After a multi-year price slide in major food names, bearish confidence by shorts has jumped (from confirmation bias of ever-declining share quotes). It's been a reinforcing circle/cycle of selling, where companies have reported weak sales and come out with lowered guidance, convincing shorts to enlarge their position sizing (both putting pressure on equity prices).

However, consumer staples in the food sector do have a storied countertrend and defensive history when the economy is close to recession and Wall Street has entered a period of bear market selling itself. So, we have these two diametrically opposed forces working each day to pressure stock quotes, one lower and one higher.

I have remained in my favorite food stocks because the valuations and dividend yields have become perhaps the most compelling of any industry in early 2026. Against a backdrop of extended valuations and investor excitement in the Big Tech area of Wall Street, food companies clearly represent the opposite setup. If your goal is to "buy low and sell high," it's easy to figure out how to handle both spaces of the market for long-term investors.

Anyway, another of my largest food holdings I have not mentioned yet on Seeking Alpha is Conagra Brands, Inc. (CAG), a leading seller of frozen meals and snacks in America (90%+ of sales originate in the U.S.). Over the past few weeks, the stock price has dipped to its lowest since 2009! Luckily, I have not owned CAG in many years, until the second half of 2025, buying at prices under $19. Over my investing career approaching 40 years, some of my best long-term performers often are blue chips bought at multi-year lows.

Read more here.

Newmont: The Silver Medal In Gold Mining, And That's Not A Bad Thing

Sidharth Kumar | Buy

Newmont Corporation (NEM) is the world's largest gold miner with multi-metal assets including copper, silver, zinc, and lead. It often suffers from comparisons with (relatively) leaner and meaner operations like Agnico and criticised for being inefficient - with increasing costs, riskier multi-jurisdictional assets, and low returns per share over the last couple of decades. While there is merit to this criticism, it overlooks the fact that an existing behemoth's playbook is necessarily different, and often singularly unique, from that of a player from a newer generation, like Agnico. Further, it looks like Newmont is wrapping up a nearly two-decade period of spending and dilution and is ready to start generating value - which is good news for investors who have been waiting a long time to see growth in this stock.

Read more here.

10% Yield Worth Considering From Rithm Capital

Colorado Wealth Management Fund | Hold

Rithm Capital (RITM) is a mortgage REIT that is more complex than many of its peers, but it has also delivered significantly better returns over long time periods. Its business model has simply worked very well. I own shares of the common stock and some of the preferred shares; specifically, I have positions in RITM-D (RITM.PR.D), RITM-E (RITM.PR.E), and RITM-F (RITM.PR.F) for the preferred shares.

Read more here.

Joby Aviation Stock Is Getting Ready For Takeoff

Bay Area Ideas | Buy

With Joby Aviation, Inc. (JOBY) being one of the leaders in the next frontier of transportation, I find it quite surprising myself that I haven't initiated coverage on the stock yet since my analysis is mostly tech focused. Today, I have finally decided to provide an analysis on the company and I believe it comes at an important time. As you can see in the stock price chart below, JOBY stock has fallen nearly 50% in the past six months. Below, I will review their most recent earnings reported in late February and also discuss some important recent developments.

Read more here.

Visa: A Rare Discount On A World Class Compounder

C Jessen | Strong Buy

Visa (V) is one of my favourite holdings, a company I’ve been long since 2016. While I’m a buy and hold kind of investor, I can say that V would be one of the very last holdings I would liquidate if I had to raise cash, it’s a forever company in my book and one I plan to add to in broader market drawbacks or whenever the opportunity presents itself as the market misprices the stock. I’m saying this as I have a strong belief this company is riding the wave of a massive growth opportunity related to the global payment ecosystem transitioning from primarily cash to digital.

Visa is one of the most lucrative network and toll booth systems on the planet, and it’s a company that has continued delivering strong growth since becoming a publicly traded company almost twenty years ago.

Read more here.

Not April Fool's, RH Is Too Cheap To Ignore

KCI Research Ltd. | Strong Buy

In The Contrarian, we have a long history with RH (RH), including my October 14th, 2016 purchases, the more recent FAM DCA purchases, various member articles over the years, including, one of my favorites published on November 22nd, 2021, titled, "Brainstorming Session 11/22/2021: Building Your Conviction & Finding The Compounders Of Capital Opportunities".

This particular article expounds on my proclamation that conviction must be earned, not borrowed, because when times get tough, and paradoxically opportunities arise, borrowed conviction will not hold up to the increased pressure and scrutiny.

Additionally, the aforementioned article hammers in the point that some of the market's most successful stocks have executed significant stock buybacks at accretive prices over the years.

Circling back, the current time frame is one where conviction is being tested, as RH shares are down roughly 18% in premarket trading, after already being down 21.9% year-to-date through yesterday, March 31st, 2026's closing price.

Read more here.

Strategy: I'm Gonna Knock It Out The Park

Oliver Rodzianko | Strong Buy

Strategy (MSTR) stock, which is down by about 10% since my last analysis of the company, is ripe for accumulation upon my further analysis. Since my February 3 piece, I have taken much more time to deep dive into Bitcoin (BTC) and what I believe is the best overall way to own exposure to the cryptocurrency: through MSTR.

Some people reading might think I was handed money on a platter; it's not true. I have been building from zero. When you start at zero, you need to look for convexity. Your claim to leadership will not be granted to you by mediocrity. It will be granted by strategy. And most of all, by meritocracy.

After my extensive research into BTC sentiment dynamics, institutional adoption, and mNAVs, and with a little help from some experts in the field, I have concluded that BTC and MSTR are currently far more attractive than gold. And the great part about BTC is that only sentiment masters can trade it with near-perfection. Old-school resource analysts will find themselves drowned in the volatility.

Read more here.

I Expect Crispr Therapeutics To Break Out As 2026 Revenue Exceeds Expectations

Robert J. Lake | Strong Buy

Crispr Therapeutics AG (CRSP), headquartered in Zug, Switzerland, is a biopharmaceutical company with a mission to "create transformative gene-based medicines for serious human diseases." With Vertex Pharmaceuticals (VRTX), they have the first and only approved gene-editing treatment on the market. It uses CRISPR/Cas-9 technology – involving precise alterations in the sequencing of a person’s genomic DNA – for the treatment of sickle cell disease (SCD) and transfusion-dependent beta thalassemia (TDT). Named Casgevy, the drug received approval by the FDA in Dec 2023 and by the EMA in Feb 2024. They also have a healthy pipeline of other drug pathways across four main areas: hemoglobinopathies, in vivo, CAR-T, and regenerative medicine.

I have covered CRSP on 3 previous occasions with ratings ranging from Buy to Strong Buy. My deep-dive on the company from October 2024 – found here – offered a comprehensive evaluation of the company and its potential at the time. For the last four years, the stock has traded mostly sideways but with some peaks around $80-90 and troughs in the mid/high $30s. The stock is currently trading at the low end of its 4-year trading range despite much of the investment risk being mitigated, post-Casgevy approval. CRSP is now 78% below its all-time high in 2021, when it was arguably overpriced, but 40% below its 12-month high reached 6 months ago. The overall market has dipped since then, of course, but CRSP, as a volatile stock, has fallen with it and in more pronounced fashion. Despite being almost 2 and a half years past approval of Casgevy, its long treatment cycle has delayed any revenue generation, but this is set to change in the next few months as revenue ramps.

I believe they are soon to reach an inflection point as they realize their revenue-generating potential with Casgevy and sustain their strong progress in their pipeline. I expect a breakout soon.

Read more here.

Kratos Defense: Leading The Charge In Hypersonics And Defense Tech

Melissa Tucker | Buy

I have covered Kratos Defense & Security Solutions (KTOS) before, where I outlined the investment thesis in detail and explained why I considered it slightly overvalued. Since then, the stock has dipped around 35%, while the inflection point regarding hypersonic missiles is closer and the company is beating earnings estimates. This makes me to upgrade the company to buy.

Read more here.

SCHG: Strong Companies At An Attractive Valuation

The Efficient Investor | Buy

The Schwab U.S. Large-Cap Growth ETF (SCHG) is a passively managed exchange-traded fund (ETF) that aims to provide exposure to the growth segment of U.S. large-cap equities. This exposure is achieved by tracking the Dow Jones U.S. Large-Cap Growth Total Stock Market Index with meaningful concentration across technology and communication services.

SCHG is down 13% year-to-date and 16.7% from its all-time high, primarily due to multiple compression. This is due to rising macroeconomic uncertainty stemming from geopolitical tensions as well as tariff policy uncertainties. That pressure has created an attractive entry point for SCHG, in my opinion. The fund’s top holdings all have strong revenue growth, expanding profit margins, and expected strong forward earnings growth. As a result, I rate SCHG a Buy.

Read more here.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Rates Spark: As The Dust Settles, There's Still A Price To Pay
data.description:
By Padhraic Garvey, CFA, Regional Head of Research, Americas; Michiel Tukker, Senior UK & Eurozone Rates Strategist; and Benjamin Schroeder, Senior Rates Strategist

The dust settles on the craziness of the past 24-48 hours, and talk now rumbles on

Our reading of events, as they unfold, points to the logic of upholding the Iran war ceasefire and an extension through to a virtual end to these specific hostilities. The US will want to get its hands on Iran's enriched uranium, and if they do, they may then hold their nose as Iran assumes a 'monitoring' role over the Strait (even if no more than a covert thing).

The market reaction to all of this was predictable: risk-on and generally relief trades. For the rates market, front ends are under less rate hike pressure, and in the US, the rate-cut story for later in 2026 is very much in play (has remained our view).

For longer maturities, it's more opaque. The reality is, we are still left with an elevated inflation dynamic through the coming number of months, and that remains an issue for inflation-unprotected fixed income products.

The impact reaction of the ceasefire news has seen yields fall right across curves, but the lingering effect risks a subsequent re-elevation in longer tenor yields, steepening curves from both ends. The 5yr area of the US curve has been re-richening, in line with a rate cut expectation, but that often co-exists with overall curve steepening.

Markets are retracing some of the rate hikes, but the growth impact may be more lasting

With oil trading well below $100 again, rate markets have more room to breathe, and so do central banks. At first sight, rates are simply following the playbook from the past month and are returning to the state when oil was at these lower levels before. This is in line with the idea that any inflation triggered by oil will only be short-lived with limited second-order effects.

As a consequence, markets are less set on European Central Bank or Bank of England rate hikes already in April, which should help mitigate rate volatility in the near term. Central banks will be reluctant to hike but at the same time cannot risk a perceived policy error.

With near-term hiking expectations smothered for the time being, central bankers will have a bit more time to decide on the next move. In effect, this helps reduce the risk of a surge in longer rates if central banks are perceived to be reacting too slowly, resulting in a further rise in inflation expectations.

But when we dive deeper into the curve, we can see that the move is not fully symmetrical. The EUR and GBP 2s5s curves remain flatter than before, which suggests that the medium-term growth outlook could face a more lasting hit. Because as the belly of the curve outperforms, markets are flagging a need for monetary easing to address growth concerns in the period after the initial inflation shock.

Front-end is retracing on lower oil prices, but the belly of the curve suggests a still worsened growth outlook

Thursday’s events and market views

All eyes remain on the Middle East and whether the ceasefire can hold. Data is less relevant, especially when it pertains to the month of February, such as the US personal spending and income data and the PCE deflator, the latter usually the Fed’s preferred measure of inflation.

We will also get the final US GDP reading for the fourth quarter of 2025. At best, this data can give a better sense of the economy’s starting point going into the crisis. The weekly jobless claims data is the only more contemporaneous release, and it comes on the heels of last Friday’s strong jobs report.

On the European side, German and Spanish industrial production data may get some attention. But most want to listen to what ECB officials are saying now that geopolitical situations no longer look as dire. Scheduled to speak is the Dutch central bank’s Sleijpen.

In primary markets, Finland is selling a new 10y benchmark via syndication. Other European supply will come from Spain, which reopens 3y, 5y and 10y bonds as well as an 11y inflation-linked bond. But also in the broader SSA space, it appears issuers are using the window of opportunity, with also KFW (5y green), COE (7y social) and UNEDIC (6y social) having mandated bond sales.

Elsewhere, the UK will auction 7y gilts (£4bn) and the US Treasury will auction 30y bonds (US$22bn).

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more
